coredata.dc:title: Synergetic effect of CQD and oxygen vacancy to TiO2 photocatalyst for boosting visible photocatalytic NO removal

Liu, Guanyu

coredata.dc:description: The wide band gap of TiO2 photocatalyst material limits its application in the field of visible photocatalysis. In this paper, oxygen vacancies and carbon quantum dots (CQD) with up-conversion character were proposed to improve the photocatalytic activity for NO removal of TiO2 under visible light irradiation. The one-dimensional TiO2 nanotube (TNs), TNs containing oxygen vacancies (OVTNs), TNs of composite CQD (CQD-TNs) and OVTNs of composite CQD (CQD-OVTNs) were prepared, respectively. Furthermore, the influence of oxygen vacancies and CQD on the removal of NOx by photocatalysis were explored. It is found that CQD-OVTNs exhibits the conspicuous synergetic effect of CQD and oxygen vacancy to boost visible photocatalytic NO removal, the NO removal efficiency was about 12, 2, and 2.6 times to TNs, OVTNs and CQD-TNs. Also, CQD-OVTNs exhibits the NO2-inhibited property during the process of photocatalytic NO removal. Finally, the synergetic mechanism of CQD and oxygen vacancies to TNs for boosting visible photocatalytic NO removal was revealed.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The wide band gap of TiO2 photocatalyst material limits its application in the field of visible photocatalysis. In this paper, oxygen vacancies and carbon quantum dots (CQD) with up-conversion character were proposed to improve the photocatalytic activity for NO removal of TiO2 under visible light irradiation. The one-dimensional TiO2 nanotube (TNs), TNs containing oxygen vacancies (OVTNs), TNs of composite CQD (CQD-TNs) and OVTNs of composite CQD (CQD-OVTNs) were prepared, respectively. Furthermore, the influence of oxygen vacancies and CQD on the removal of NOx by photocatalysis were explored. It is found that CQD-OVTNs exhibits the conspicuous synergetic effect of CQD and oxygen vacancy to boost visible photocatalytic NO removal, the NO removal efficiency was about 12, 2, and 2.6 times to TNs, OVTNs and CQD-TNs. Also, CQD-OVTNs exhibits the NO2-inhibited property during the process of photocatalytic NO removal. Finally, the synergetic mechanism of CQD and oxygen vacancies to TNs for boosting visible photocatalytic NO removal was revealed.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:sections.ce:para: As a hazardous pollutant, NOx will induce people's lung diseases and environmental pollution such as haze, photochemical smog and the greenhouse effect. Although the effectiveness of photocatalytic technology in removing NOx has been confirmed, its catalytic efficiency is still inferior under visible light. In this paper, the high-efficient visible photocatalytic removal for NOx by TiO2 through the up-conversion effect of carbon quantum dots and the construction of oxygen vacancy was achieved, and further the mechanism of the synergy of carbon quantum dots and oxygen vacancy to promote the visible photocatalytic NOx was revealed.

ce:section.ce:para: As a major pollutant in industrial production and vehicle exhaust, NO will induce people's lung diseases and environmental pollution such as haze, photochemical smog and the greenhouse effect [38,55]. Photocatalysis is considered as a promising technology for NO removal due to its advantages of low cost, high efficiency, mild reaction conditions and environmental friendliness [5,18]. As a typical photocatalytic material, TiO2 photocatalysts are one of the most suitable photocatalytic materials for engineering applications due to their non-toxic, excellent photocatalytic activity, strong versatility, stable physical and chemical characteristics [15,16]. While TiO2 photocatalysts with a wide bandgap can only absorb ultraviolet light accounting for about 4 % of the solar spectrum. It is difficult to exert photocatalytic activity for NO removal by TiO2 under visible light irradiation, which is the main factor hindering its photocatalytic effect in practical applications.

Therefore, various research on bandgap regulation of TiO2 photocatalysts [22, 29,52] and new visible light photocatalytic materials (dominated by C3N4 and Bi-based photocatalysts et al.) [21,4,44] have swarming emerged to realize photocatalytic NO removal under visible light irradiation. Tang et al. compounded the long afterglow material with C3N4 and confirmed its effectiveness in NO removal [51]. Dong et al. used ultra-thin two-dimensional Bi-based materials for the photocatalytic removal of NO and explored the influence of the structural design on the photocatalytic degradation performance of NO, which laid the foundation for Bi-based materials to effectively remove NO [28]. Although such new visible photocatalysts exhibit a certain photocatalytic activity under visible light irradiation, it is still difficult to be applied to practical projects due to their inferior stability and uncertain photocatalytic activity [32,50,58].

The fabrication technology of oxygen vacancies, ion doping and heterostructure can effectively improve the visible photocatalytic activity of TiO2 photocatalytic materials [35]. Wherein the introduction of oxygen vacancy active centers has attracted much attention due to that effectively improves visible light responsiveness, inhibits electron-hole recombination and NO2 production in the NO removal process [39,41]. The regulation of bandgap structure for TiO2 can effectively broaden its light response range, while its visible photocatalytic activity is still unsatisfactory [23].

The proposal of carbon quantum dots (CQD) and rare earth up-conversion luminescent materials exploit new ideas to improve the visible photocatalytic activity of UV-responsive photocatalysts such as TiO2 [19]. Anti-stokes luminescence characteristics of up-conversion luminescent materials can be excited by light of long wavelength (visible light, infrared light, low energy) and then emit light of short wavelength (ultraviolet light, high energy) [8]. CQD has been widely applied in photocatalysts due to its photoluminescence properties, low toxicity and resistance to photobleaching [11]. Martins et al. prepared N-CQD/P25 by hydrothermal method, which exhibited an improvement in photocatalytic activity under visible irradiation [31]. Qin et al. found that CQD promoted the ability of P25 for photocatalytic NO removal under visible light [34]. These studies have laid a research foundation for the application of CQD in the field of photocatalytic NO removal.

However, few studies have reported the oxygen vacancies-containing TiO2 photocatalyst compounded with CQD for photocatalytic NO removal, and the interaction behavior between CQD and active oxygen vacancies is indistinct. Here, 0D CQD and 1D TiO2 photocatalysts (TNs) were prepared by hydrothermal method, and TNs containing oxygen vacancies (OVTNs) were obtained by reducing TNs with sodium borohydride. And then CQD-OVTNs was fabricated by CQD solution and OVTNs through a wet chemical method. The photocatalytic activity for NO removal of CQD-OVTNs was investigated by the NO photocatalytic system under full spectrum and visible light. Furthermore, the possible reasons for the enhanced photocatalytic activity of CQD-OVTNs have been discussed combined with its crystal structure, morphology, optical properties, and the synergistic effect of CQD and oxygen vacancies. Finally, the synergetic mechanism of CQD and oxygen vacancy to TNs for boosting visible photocatalytic NO removal was revealed based on excitation and optical absorption characteristics, the density functional theory (DFT) calculation, photo-generated active radicals, band structure analysis and photocatalytic NO pathway.

Citric acid (98 %), urea (98 %), and sodium hydroxide (NaOH, 96 %) were purchased from Tianli Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (China, Tianjin). TiO2 (P25, 80 % anatase, and 20 % rutile) was purchased from Lijie Chemical Co., Ltd (China, Shaoxing). Hydrochloric acid (37 %) was purchased from Haohua Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (China, Beijing). Absolute ethanol (99 %) was purchased from Fuyu Fine Chemical Co., Ltd (China, Tianjin). Sodium borohydride (NaBH4, 98 %) was purchased from Jingbo Biotechnology Co., Ltd (China, Xi’an).

CQD and TNs were synthesized by the hydrothermal method [17,24]. Typically, 3.0 g citric acid and 1.0 g urea were fully dissolved in 10 mL deionized water, and then the solution was added to a 50 mL polytetrafluoroethylene reactor. After hydrothermal reaction at 180 °C for 5 h, it was taken out and centrifuged to obtain a suspension, and the suspension was diluted 20 times to obtain CQD suspension. Meanwhile, 1.0 g of TiO2 photocatalyst (P25) was added to 10 M NaOH solution, and hydrothermal reacted at 150 ℃ in a reactor for 24 h to obtain the suspension. And then the suspension was washed with water to neutral. Afterward, the obtained suspension was adjusted pH to 2 with 0.1 M HCl solution and magnetically stirred for 24 h at 500 rpm. Following, the suspension was washed to neutral, and then the sediment was dispersed in ethanol and continued stirring for 24 h. Finally, the suspension was dried and then calcined at 550 ℃ for 2 h to obtain TNs. To obtain OVTNs, 1.0 g TNs was mixed with 0.5 g NaBH4 and added into the porcelain crucible, calcined at 350 °C for 1 h. The powders after calcining were washed with deionized water and absolute ethanol 3–4 times and dried.

Furthermore, 0.5 g TNs or OVTNs were dispersed in 20 mL absolute ethanol, a certain volume of CQD solution was added during vigorous stirring immediately, and the mixture was stirred at a constant temperature of 80 ℃ until volatilization was completed. Then, the powders were added to a muffle furnace and calcined at 300 ℃ for 3 h to obtain CQD-TNs or CQD-OVTNs. The specific preparation process is shown in Fig. 1. Wherein, 3.125 mL, 6.25 mL, 12.5 mL and 25.0 mL CQD solutions are selected to prepare CQD-OVTNs, which are named CQD1-OVTNs, CQD2-OVTNs, CQD3-OVTNs and CQD4-OVTNs.

Up-conversion characteristics of CQD were characterized by a Fluorescence spectrometer (FluoroLog3, Jobin Yvon, HORIBA, America) at the different excitation wavelengths. Micromorphology of photocatalysts was investigated by transmission electron microscope (TEM, Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN, FEI, America) and scanning electron microscope (SEM, S-4800, Hitachi, 15.0 kV, Japan). Nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms and BET specific surface area (S BET) were detected by micromeritics physisorption, (ASAP 2020, Micromeritics, America). Microstructure and chemical bond state of photocatalysts were detected by X-ray diffraction analysis (XRD, D8ADVANCE, Bruker, Germany, Cu-Kα operating at 40 kV and 40 mA) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, AXIS ULTRA, Kratos, Britain). Optical properties were measured by a UV-Vis spectrophotometer (UV3600, Shimadzu Corporation, Japan) equipped with an integrated sphere. Photogenerated electrons and electron transfer properties of photocatalysts were evaluated by a Fluorescence spectrometer and an electrochemical workstation (CHI660E, Shanghai Chenhua Instrument Co., Ltd, China) under the irradiation of a Xe arc lamp light (PLS-SXE 300, Beijing Perfectlight Technology Co.,Ltd., China). NO2 - and NO3 - were tested by ion chromatograph (DIONEX AQUION, Thermo Fisher, America).

DFT calculation was employed through the Vienna ab initio simulation package (VASP) with the Perdew-Burke-Ernzerhofer (PBE). The cut-off energy was adopted 400 eV and the Monkorst-Pack K-points (111) was applied in the Brillouin zone. The adsorption energy (E ads) was calculated as: E ads = E total - E slab - E mole, where E total, E slab and E mole depict the total energy of the adsorption complex, the pristine photocatalysts, and the isolated molecule (NO), respectively. Photo-generated free radicals were characterized by an electron paramagnetic resonance spectrometer (EPR, BrukerE500, Bruker, Germany).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Test method of photocatalytic activity for NO removal
ce:section.ce:para:
The photocatalytic activity for NO removal was conducted by a Gas phase catalytic system (CEL-GPCR100, Beijing China Education Au-light Technology Co., Ltd, China). The diagram of the photocatalytic system was shown in Fig. S1. A stable NO gas source is provided through 50 ppm NO standard gas (2 mL/min) and compressed air (98 mL/min). A 500 W Xenon lamp (CEL-WLAX500, Beijing China Education Au-light Technology Co., Ltd, China) was used to provide full spectrum irradiation, and the visible light was obtained by installing a 420 nm cutoff filter. Light intensity was about 58 mW/cm2 on the surface of photocatalysts. 75 mg photocatalyst was selected and placed in the photocatalytic reactor as the active sample. And then, the full spectrum or visible light source was turned on until the initial concentration of NO in the reaction chamber stabilized at 1 ppm. The steady flow rate was 100 mL/min, the temperature and relative humidity were controlled at 25 ℃ and 50 %. Also, the real-time concentrations of NO, NO2 and NO were regularly recorded through a NO-NO2-NOx analyzer (42i, Thermo Fisher, America). Furthermore, The NO removal efficiency (η), the conversion efficiency of NO to NO2 (α) and NO to NO3 - (γ) were calculated as Eqs. 1–3.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Test method of in situ DRIFTS investigation
ce:section.ce:para: The spectroscopy of adsorption and photocatalytic reaction for NO were investigated by an in-situ diffuse Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (DRIFTS) apparatus (TENSOR II, Bruker). The samples were pretreated at 150 ℃ for 60 min in He atmosphere to remove the impurities. Then, NO (50 mL/min) and O2 (50 mL/min) were injected into the reactor. The in-situ DRIFTS spectroscopy was detected regularly in the dark and visible light irradiation.

To explore the up-conversion characteristics of CQD, the detailed photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra were carried out by using different excitation wavelengths. The up-conversion emission behavior of CQD exhibits an excitation-dependence, the emission wavelengths are located in the range of 350–550 nm ( Fig. 2). With the continuous red shift of the excitation wavelength, no obvious changes are observed at the emission wavelength, but the emission intensity increases significantly. In general, the up-conversion behavior of CQD can effectively convert near-infrared and visible light (long wavelength, low-energy) into ultraviolet and near-ultraviolet light (short wavelength, high-energy), which is more conducive to the activation of ultraviolet-responded photocatalysts under visible irradiation.

Fig. 3 shows the SEM and TEM images of photocatalysts. A “catkin” structure agglomerated by many nanoscale one-dimensional structures exhibited by TNs, OVTNs, CQD-TNs and CQD-OVTNs in Fig. 3(a-d). To clearly observe the morphology and the composite of CQD and OVTNs, TEM tests were carried out on TNs and CQD-OVTNs, as shown in Fig. 3(e-h). TNs present typical bamboo-like one-dimensional nanotube structures with smooth surface, shallow interior and dense boundary colors. Similar morphology can also be found in the TEM images of CQD-OVTNs, which indicates that the one-dimensional nanostructures are maintained after the introduction of oxygen vacancies and CQD. To clearly observe the morphology and the composite of CQD, high-resolution TEM tests were carried out on CQD-OVTNs, as shown in Fig. 3g, h. Many black spots are scattered on the surface of nanoscale one-dimensional structures in the morphology of CQD-OVTNs, which demonstrates the successful compounding of CQD on the surface of OVTNs.

The textural properties and S BET of photocatalysts are important factors affecting the photocatalytic activity [36], which were characterized by nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms, S BET and pore analysis, as shown in Fig. 3i and Table 1. All samples exhibited the typical type IV isotherms with H3 hysteretic loops, which indicates that the mesopores structure exists in photocatalysts [52,53]. S BET and pore volume of TNs is 223.55 m2/g and 0.92 cm3/g, while S BET and pore volume increase to 323.32 m2/g and 1.39 cm3/g after the introduction of oxygen vacancies. This may be attributed to the formation of defect structures and unsaturated sites induced by oxygen vacancies [27]. After the compound of CQD, the S BET and pore volume of CQD-TNs (208.56 m2/g, 0.86 cm3/g) and CQD-OVTNs (251.28 m2/g, 1.25 cm3/g) decreased compared to TNs and OVTNs. This is mainly because that the filling and agglomerate effect on the texture and structure of pristine TNs and OVTNs. It can also be seen from the pore-diameter distribution curve (the inset of Fig. 3e), the overall pore volume decreased, and the average pore diameter has become larger after the introduction of CQD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Microstructure and chemical composition of photocatalysts
ce:section.ce:para: The microstructure and chemical composition of photocatalysts were characterized by XRD and XPS. All four photocatalysts exhibit similar diffraction peaks ( Fig. 4a), which are located at 25.3◦ [101], 37.9◦ [004], 48.0◦ [200], 54.0◦ [105], 55.1◦ [211] and 62.8◦ [204] corresponding to the anatase phase of TiO2 [3,7]. No obvious changes in cell parameters (Table S1) and diffraction peaks with the introduction of oxygen vacancies and CQD, which indicates that oxygen vacancies and CQD would not affect the crystal structure of TNs. This may be due to the amorphous structure and uniform dispersion of CQD on the surface of TNs and OVTNs. Differently, the crystallite sizes of TNs have slightly decreased with the introduction of oxygen vacancies and the recombination of carbon quantum dots, which is mainly due to the defect structure caused by oxygen vacancies and the CQD hindering the growth crystal size [33,42,52].

High-resolution XPS spectra of Ti (b), O (c) and C (d) of photocatalysts were shown in Fig. 4b-d. The main peaks of Ti 2p1/2, Ti 2p3/2 appeared at 464.2 eV and 458.5 eV, illustrating that Ti4+ is the major form of Ti in TNs, OVTNs and CQD-OVTNs [25]. It is worth noting that after the introduction of oxygen vacancies, a peak of strong oxidative Ti3+ at 460.2 eV appeared [10]. This mainly implies the existence of oxygen vacancies and the lattice defects that may be caused by oxygen vacancies. The oxygen vacancies also can be further confirmed by EPR characterization (Fig. S2). OVTNs and CQD-OVTNs show an intense symmetrical EPR signal of oxygen vacancies at g = 2.003 [54], while this signal was not found in TNs. This shows that oxygen vacancies can be effectively introduced into the lattice of TNs through the addition of sodium borohydride.

The peaks at 529.8 eV and 531.7 eV of O 1s can be attributed to the O2- anions of TNs and the hydroxyl oxygen peak of ∙OH generated on the surface of TNs [47,48]. As shown in Fig. 4d, three typical peaks at 284.8 eV, 286.0 eV and 288.4 eV can assign to the C-C or CC, C-O and CO bond of CQD itself and the chemical interaction with OVTNs [23–25, 34].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical, electronic separation and migration properties of photocatalysts
ce:section.ce:para: The optical properties, the separation and migration of electrons in photocatalysts are the key factors to determine the photocatalytic activity [49]. Fig. 5a shows the UV-Vis absorbance spectrum. It can be seen that the light absorption intensity of OVTNs is increased compared with TNs, and the light-absorption limit is red shifted, which is mainly caused by the defect energy-level structure between the conduction band and valence band provided by oxygen vacancies [39]. Besides, the absorption intensity of CQD-TNs and CQD-OVTNs in the visible light region has been significantly improved. This can be rationalized by the up-conversion feature, surface sensitization and the color change of the photocatalyst after compounding CQD [30,43]. It can also be seen from the spectrum of bandgap calculated by the Kubelka-Munk function transformation (Fig. 5b), the calculated bandgap (E g) of TNs, OVTNs, CQD-TNs and CQD-OVTNs are 3.41, 3.09, 2.79 and 2.62 eV, respectively. This indicated that both CQD and oxygen vacancies can narrow the bandgap of the TiO2 photocatalyst. Furthermore, the bandgap becomes smaller when they coexist, which is more conducive to the activation of photocatalyst activity stimulated by visible light.

Also, the separation and migration of electrons in photocatalysts were characterized by PL spectra and photocurrent response, as shown in Fig. 5c and d. The PL intensity of CQD-OVTNs and CQD-TNs are significantly lower than OVTNs and TNs, indicating that CQD as an electron mediator in the photocatalytic system promotes the separation and transfer of photogenerated electrons and reduces the recombination rate [6]. Besides, OVTNs also showed relatively low PL intensity, suggesting that defects structure induced by oxygen vacancies effectively promote the excitation and separation of photogenerated electrons within OVTNs. The same trend can be seen in the photocurrent response (Fig. 5d), CQD-OVTNs shows the strongest photocurrent response, followed by CQD-TNs and OVTNs, while TNs has a weak photocurrent response. This indicates that the introduction of CQD and oxygen vacancies has a synergistic effect to improve the separation and transfer of electrons and holes. Besides, the photocurrent responses of both TNs and OVTNs showed attenuation with time, while CQD-TNs and CQD-OVTNs remained basically stable, indicating that CQD as an electron mediator can effectively improve the lifetime and stability of photogenerated electrons.

To determine the optimal dosage of CQD, photocatalytic NO removal tests under visible irradiation were carried out on a series of OVTNs with different CQD amounts, the results are shown in Fig. S3. CQD3-OVTNs exhibits the most excellent NO removal efficiency, which is mainly due to that the appropriate amount of CQD, not only improves the visible response of the photocatalysis system in visible light but also effectively improves the electron-hole separation characteristics. While, insufficient CQD incorporation is difficult to meet the adequate up-conversion efficiency, so the NO removal efficiency of CQD1-OVTNs and CQD2-OVTNs is inferior. However, excessive CQD exists in the photocatalysis system, which will may shield the active sites for NO removal and become the composite center of electrons and holes, so the removal efficiency of CQD4-OVTNs is also significantly lower than CQD3-OVTNs [34]. Accordingly, CQD3-OVTNs with excellent NO removal efficiency is selected as the CQD-OVTNs photocatalyst to conduct follow-up research.

The time-series concentrations of NO, NO2 and NOx under full spectrum irradiation are presented in Fig. 6, the NO removal efficiency (η), the conversion efficiency of NO to NO2 (α) and NO to NO3 - (γ) during the photocatalytic test for NO removal are summarized in Table S2. CQD-OVTNs shows the most excellent ability for NO removal (η = 57.8 %), meanwhile has a lower NO2 production (α = 1.4 %), which exhibits the highest NOx removal rate (γ = 57.0 %). Also, OVTNs acts as good NO and NOx removal effects (γ = 53.1 %)., and the conversion efficiency of NO to NO2 (α = 2.4 %) is slightly higher than CQD-OVTNs. However, the NOx removal effects of TNs and CQD-TNs are relatively inferior and produce a large amount of NO2. This demonstrates that the introduction of oxygen vacancies effectively improves the NO photocatalytic efficiency and suppresses the generation of toxic NO2. While the photocatalytic activity of CQD-TNs with an only composite of CQD has not been significantly improved under the full spectrum, η = 46.1 %. Therefore, the most excellent photocatalytic activity exhibited by CQD-OVTNs suggests that CQD and oxygen vacancies may exhibit excellent synergy to photocatalytic NO removal in the photocatalytic system.

Under visible light irradiation, TNs basically has no photocatalytic NOx removal effect ( Fig. 7a-c). The concentration of NO only decreases by about 40 ppb (η = 4.0 %), while the concentration of NO2 generated is about 10 ppb. This may be due to the wide band gap of TNs, which results in its light response range being mainly in the ultraviolet light region (Fig. 5a) and difficult to be excited by light sources larger than 420 nm. The changes in NOx concentration are mainly due to the natural oxidation of NO in the air environment [13,26,27]. Furthermore, OVTNs shows good photocatalytic effects (η = 23.6 %) for NO removal under visible light irradiation, which indicates that the change of bandgap structure caused by oxygen vacancies can effectively expand the light response range to above 420 nm. For CQD-TNs, the up-conversion effect of CQD can effectively upconvert visible light with a wavelength greater than 420 nm into ultraviolet and near-ultraviolet light with a range of 350–550 nm (according to Fig. 2), which can effectively excite TNs. Thus CQD-TNs can also show certain photocatalytic removal NO performance under visible light irradiation, η = 17.6 %.

When CQD and oxygen vacancies were co-exiting, the visible photocatalytic activity of CQD-OVTNs (η = 47.0 %, α = 1.2 % and γ = 46.4 %) was significantly higher than that of OVTNs and CQD-TNs. This is mainly because the up-conversion characteristics of CQD exert an exceptional synergistic effect with the expansion of the light response range caused by the defect structure induced by oxygen vacancies. So, the utilization of visible light and photocatalytic activity by CQD-OVTNs greatly improves. Besides, the CQD-OVTNs proposed with the synergistic effect of CQD and oxygen vacancies exhibited comparable visible photocatalytic NO removal effect compared to the reported research (Table S4). The NO removal efficiency of CQD-OVTNs (47 %) under visible light is about 12 times that of pure TiO2 (4 %). The outstanding NO removal effect and NO2-inhibited property of CQD-OVTNs provide a greener photocatalytic process, which lays a robust foundation for the development of this material in the field of photocatalysis for NOx removal.

In addition, the stability of photocatalysis is an important indicator to evaluate the photocatalytic capacity of photocatalysts [1,9]. In order to effectively investigate the photocatalytic stability, CQD-OVTNs were measured through a cyclic photocatalytic NO removal test, and the results are shown in Fig. 8. It can be observed that with the increase in the number of cycles, the NO removal rate decreased slightly. This is mainly caused by the continuous accumulation of NO degradation products (nitrate) on the surface of CQD-OVTNs. Previous studies have reported that nitric acid, as the final degradation product of NO, will further arise disproportionation on the surface of photocatalysts and release toxic NO2 gas [2,12]. Whereas the concentration of NO2 basically remains stable with the increase in the number of tests, which illustrates that although the accumulation of nitrate will cover the CQD-OVTNs surface active sites to a certain extent, the existing form is relatively stable. This can be regarded as CQD-OVTNs displays more green and environmental characteristics in the photocatalytic removal of NO.

Furthermore, the XRD pattern and SEM images of CQD-OVTNs before and after the cycle test is shown in Fig. 8b and Fig. S4. Fresh and used CQD-TNs show basically the same diffraction peak and morphology. This may be due to that the concentration of photocatalytic product (NO3 -) formed by the lower NO concentration (1 ppm) under the photocatalysis is tiny and evenly distributed on the surface of the photocatalysts, which does not affect the chemical composition, crystal structure and micro-morphology. This phenomenon further confirms the stability of the photocatalysts and provides a guarantee in practical engineering applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synergetic mechanism of CQD and oxygen vacancy for boosting photocatalytic NO removal
ce:section.ce:para: To further clearly explore the synergistic mechanism of CQD and oxygen vacancy in CQD-OVTNs for boosting photocatalytic NO, the up-conversion excited photocatalytic properties, adsorption and reaction free energy, reaction active radicals and pathway of photocatalytic NO were investigated by UV-Vis and PL spectra, DFT calculation, EPR and in-situ DRIFTS spectra.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Excitation and optical absorption characteristics under visible light
ce:section.ce:para: It can be found in Fig. 9, the emission wavelengths of CQD exciting by typical visible light irradiation (wavelength at 500, 600, 700 and 800 nm) are located in the range of 350–550 nm. Furthermore, the light absorption limit of CQD-OVTNs has a significant red-shift due to the band gap regulation and photosensitization, which can effectively absorb light of about 250–600 nm and be activated to photocatalytic reaction. Therefore, the absorption property of CQD-OVTNs has a significant coupling effect with the up-conversion emission characteristic of CQD due to the synergies of bandgap regulation by oxygen vacancy and up-conversion of CQD. The purple shadow in Fig. 9 is the photoexcitation region to provoke the photocatalysis for NO removal of CQD-OVTNs under visible light irradiation theoretically. In general, the synergies of bandgap regulation by oxygen vacancy and up-conversion of CQD can greatly improve the photocatalytic activity in visible light from the perspective of the optical property.

ce:section.ce:section-title: DFT Calculation for photocatalytic NO adsorption and activation
ce:section.ce:para: In order to better understand the effect of CQD and oxygen vacancy on photocatalytic NO removal, the adsorption energy and reaction-free energy of NO on different photocatalytic materials were further explored by DFT calculation. Fig. S5 reveals the configuration model of TNs, OVTNs CQD-TNs and CQD-OVTNs. It can be seen that the free energy changes for the optimal configurations of CQD-TNs and CQD-OVTNs are −2.92 and −8.64 eV, respectively. The lower adsorption energy (−8.64 eV) for the optimal configurations of CQD-OVTNs may imply that CQD prefers to compound the photocatalytic system with constructing oxygen vacancy, and the existing form is stable.

The NO adsorption configurations were established on the surface of TNs, OVTNs, CQD-TNs and CQD-OVTNs optimal configurations, as shown in Fig. 10a-d. Fig. 10e shows the optimal NO adsorption configuration of different photocatalysts, in which NO adsorption energy (−1.99 eV) on the surface of TNs is the weakest. The NO adsorption energy of OVTNs and CQD-TNs are −3.38 and −2.48 eV, illustrating that CQD and oxygen vacancies effectively improve the adsorption characteristics of NO. Also, the Bader charge analysis shows the same regularity, wherein the Bader charge of NO on TNs, OVTNs and CQD-TNs are −1.18e, −1.40e and −1.38e, respectively. Interestingly, the adsorption energies of OVTNs and CQD-TNs differ greatly, while Bader charges are relatively close. This mainly attributes to the oxygen vacancies induced defect structure can effectively improve the adsorption of NO, and CQD as an electronic medium can promote the electrons transfer between photocatalyst and NO. Furthermore, CQD-OVTNs with the largest NO adsorption energy (−4.23 eV) and Bader charge (−1.72 eV) exhibits the synergy of CQD and oxygen vacancy to adsorption and activation of NO. This implies that NO is more easily adsorbed and activated on the surface of CQD-OVTNs, which theoretically explains its outstanding photocatalytic performance for NO removal.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Active radicals and band structure analysis
ce:section.ce:para: To further investigate the synergetic mechanism of CQD and oxygen vacancy for boosting photocatalytic NO removal, the main reaction radicals (∙OH and ∙O2 -) under visible light irradiation were detected by EPR spectra as shown in Fig. 11a, b. Obviously, OVTNs, CQD-TNs and CQD-OVTNs photocatalysts all produce a large amount of hydroxyl and superoxide radicals after 10 min of visible light irradiation, except for TNs, which corresponds to the results of previous photocatalytic NO removal (Fig. 7). It is worth noting that CQD-OVTNs exhibits stronger EPR intensity than OVTNs and CQD-TNs, indicating that more strong oxidation active radicals are generated, mainly because of the optimal visible light utilization efficiency and high electron separation characteristics due to the synergistic effect of CQD and oxygen vacancies.

The generation of active radicals for photocatalytic NO can also be verified from the bandgap structure analysis of the sample. The valence band (VB) values were 2.21, 2.13, 2.31 and 2.25 eV of TNs, OVTNs, CQD-TNs and CQD-OVTNs, respectively (Fig. 11c), which are more positive than the potential required for H2O to be oxidized to ∙OH (1.99 eV) [56]. This indicates that the four photocatalysts can be excited by light to produce a large number of ∙OH active radicals. Furthermore, the conduction band (CB) was calculated according to E CB = E VB - E g [47], which is −1.20, −0.96, −0.39 and −0.36 eV of TNs, OVTNs, CQD-TNs and CQD-OVTNs (Fig. 11d) and fulfill the required conversion potential from O2 to O2 - (−0.33 eV) [20,40]. It can be concluded that although the introduction of CQD and oxygen vacancy narrows the band gap of TNs, there is still enough potential to effectively generate the main active species required for photocatalytic NO through a redox reaction. Besides, the defect structure and the electronic medium formed by oxygen vacancy and CQD promote the separation and transfer of electrons and holes, which makes abundant electrons and holes accumulate in the conduction band and valence band of CQD-OVTNs, resulting in that more ∙OH and ∙O2 - active radicals are generated.

In-suit DRIFTS spectra were conducted to monitor the adsorption and reaction pathways of NO at CQD-OVTNs surface, as shown in Fig. 12. The assignment of peaks in in-suit DRIFTS spectra of adsorption and photocatalytic NO was displayed in Table S4. The bands of NO, N2O2 (1785, 1741, 1359 and 1347 cm-1) appear and increase during the prolonging of NO adsorption. Also, the bands appear at 1614, 1446, 1286 and 1258 cm-1, which are assigned to NO2 and N2O4, NO2 -, and NO3 -, indicating that a slow NO oxidation arises without light irradiation in the oxygen-rich environment [13,14]. It is worth noting that the band of NO is significantly enhanced after visible light irradiation, which may be caused by the synergies of oxygen vacancies defects and CQD, which promotes the adsorption and charges interaction between NO and the active sites of CQD-OVTNs. The strong adsorption for NO can also be seen in the DFT results (Fig. 10).

Furthermore, new bands of NO3 - (1648 and 1536 cm-1) are generated and the intensity is increasing with the visible light irradiation, while the intensity of the bands assigning to NO2 (1614 cm-1) and NO2 - (1446 cm-1) are relatively weak. This demonstrated that the main photocatalytic products of NO removal are NO3 -. NO2 and NO2 - mainly exist on the surface of the photocatalyst as intermediate products, which indicated the eco-friendly progress during photocatalytic NO by CQD-OVTNs. More importantly, NO+ as the intermediate species was surveyed at 2241 cm-1, which is conducive to the direct photocatalytic reaction of NO into nitrate, and the production of intermediate toxic NO2 and nitrite is avoided [37,45]. Furthermore, the generated NOx- has been tested by ion chromatograph (DIONEX AQUION, Thermo Fisher, America) to quantitative analyze the photocatalytic products. The results were showed in Fig. S6 and Table S5. It can be found that there is an obvious NO3 - ion characteristic peak at 8.157 min, while the characteristic peak corresponding to NO2 - ion does not appear at 6.71 min. This shows that the main product of photocatalytic removal of NO is NO3 -, which is also consistent with the results of in-suit DRIFTS spectra. Based on the above discussion, in-suit DRIFTS and EPR results, the photocatalytic NO pathways of CQD-OVTNs are summarized and displayed in Fig. 12b:

According to the results of the photocatalytic test for NO removal and in-suit DRIFTS spectra, CQD-OVTNs exhibits high NO to NO3 - removal rates and NO2 inhibition characteristics. To further explore the influence of CQD and oxygen vacancies on the NO photocatalytic removal pathway, DFT calculations were carried out on the free energy changes of NO, intermediate (NO2) and final products (NO3 -) in the process of photocatalytic NO over different photocatalysts. As shown in Fig. 12c, CQD-OVTNs shows a larger free energy difference of NO to NO3 - than TNs, OVTNs, and CQD-TNs, indicating that the conversion of NO to the final product NO3 - is thermodynamically favorable. Additionally, the free energy differences of TNs and CQD-TNs on NO to NO2 are significantly greater than OVTNs and CQD-OVTNs, implying that TNs and CQD-TNs tend to preferentially degrade NO to NO2 and then convert to NO3 - in the process of NO removal. While OVTNs and CQD-OVTNs with stronger oxidation induced by oxygen vacancies [46,57] exhibit the NO2 inhibition characteristics, which can directly oxidize NO to NO3 -.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synergetic mechanism of CQD and oxygen vacancy to photocatalytic NO
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 13 shows the synergetic mechanism of CQD and oxygen vacancy for boosting visible photocatalytic NO removal. Due to the oxygen vacancies induced defect structure and the sensitization of CQD, the bandgap of CQD-OVTNs has been significantly reduced (about 2.62 eV, Fig. 5), which can effectively extend the light excitation wavelength to the visible light region. At the same time, the received low energy, long wavelength visible and infrared light can effectively convert into a high energy, short wavelength near ultraviolet and ultraviolet light by the up-conversion of CQD, which further promotes the excitation of photocatalysis for NO removal. In addition, the special conduction band and valence band positions of CQD-OVTNs fulfill the oxidation and reduction potentials required for the generating of main active species ∙OH and ∙O2 - for photocatalytic NO oxidation sufficiently (Fig. 11). The defect structure formed by the oxygen vacancy can provide the center of electron capture, meanwhile the CQD near the oxygen vacancy as the electron transfer medium can effectively transfer electrons to the photocatalytic sites on the surface of CQD-OVTNs. This promotes the effective separation of electrons and holes and improves the utilization of photogenerated electrons and holes. On the whole, the synergetic effect of oxygen vacancy and CQD on light excitation and absorption performance, bandgap structure, electron transfer and separation characteristics improve the visible photocatalytic activity and environmental friendliness for photocatalytic NO in CQD-OVTNs.

In conclusion, the 0D CQD compounded 1D Ti-based photocatalysts containing oxygen vacancy CQD-OVTNs were synthesized by hydrothermal method. Compared to TNs, OVTNs and CQD-TNs, the synergistic effect of CQD and oxygen vacancies of CQD-OVTNs improves the photocatalytic NO activity significantly under visible light irradiation. Defect structure induced by oxygen vacancies not only optimized the band-gap, but also provide the electron capture centers, which greatly improved the separation efficiency of electrons and holes in the photocatalyst, and energized the adsorption and activation behavior of NO at the active site. The up-conversion property of CQD can effectively convert the long wavelength visible light and near-infrared light into the near ultraviolet and ultraviolet light that can excite CQD-OVTNs. Also, CQD acts as an electron transfer medium, which promotes the generation of photocatalytic NO active radicals. Furthermore, the absorption property of CQD-OVTNs has a high coupling effect with the up-conversion emission characteristic of CQD due to the synergies of band gap regulation by oxygen vacancy and up-conversion of CQD, which exhibits a synergetic effect for boosting visible photocatalytic NO removal. Additionally, CQD-OVTNs effectively inhibit the production of toxic NO2 and NO2 - substances during the photocatalytic NO process and are more inclined to convert NO into harmless nitrate. Therefore, the synergetic effect of CQD and oxygen vacancy improves the visible photocatalytic activity and environmental friendliness for photocatalytic NO removal.

Fen Li and Guanyu Liu conceived and designed the experiment, participated in the discussion of the results; Guanyu Liu drafted the manuscript; Jiayu Wu conducted a research and investigation process; Fuqiang Liu and Sanqiang Yang reviewed format and content of the manuscript.
